claims.claim_text: A British Columbia tribunal ruled in February 2024 that Air Canada was responsible for false bereavement-fare information its chatbot gave to a customer.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The incident is real, but the primary legal source is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module dates the Air Canada ruling incorrectly.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, first sentence
claims.anchor_text: In 2023, Air Canada was forced to pay damages to a customer
claims.suggested_rewrite: In February 2024, Air Canada was ordered to compensate a customer
claims.rationale: The module conflates the November 2022 customer interaction with the February 14, 2024 tribunal decision, so it cannot be supported as written.

claims.claim_text: The original RAG paper was published in 2020.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, bullet 1
claims.rationale: The arXiv record directly supports the paper title and 2020 publication date.

claims.claim_text: The module claims the original RAG paper showed a 400M retrieval model outperforming a 175B non-retrieval model.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, bullet 1
claims.anchor_text: demonstrating that a 400M parameter model with retrieval could outperform a 175B parameter model without it.
claims.suggested_rewrite: showing retrieval-augmented models could outperform strong parametric-only baselines on knowledge-intensive tasks.
claims.rationale: The cited RAG paper supports strong benchmark gains, but not this exact 400M-versus-175B comparison.

claims.claim_text: Perplexity AI generated $20 million in annual recurring revenue in 2024 entirely from commercializing a RAG architecture.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, bullet 2
claims.anchor_text: Perplexity AI generated $20 million in annual recurring revenue in 2024 entirely by commercializing a highly optimized RAG architecture.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Perplexity AI is an example of a commercial product built around retrieval-grounded answers.
claims.rationale: The exact revenue figure and the causal claim about revenue being entirely attributable to RAG are not supportable from an allowlisted primary source here.

claims.claim_text: Anthropic researchers discovered in 2023 that exactly 500 tokens yielded 85 percent retrieval recall.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, bullet 3
claims.anchor_text: Anthropic researchers discovered in 2023 that a chunk size of exactly 500 tokens yielded an 85 percent retrieval recall, outperforming both smaller and larger chunk sizes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Chunk size materially affects retrieval quality, so teams usually benchmark several chunk sizes instead of assuming one universal default.
claims.rationale: No allowlisted primary source was found for this exact Anthropic study or these exact numbers.

claims.claim_text: A standard enterprise RAG deployment costs $5,700 per month, deflects 10,000 tickets, and yields roughly 30x ROI.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, bullet 4
claims.anchor_text: A standard enterprise RAG deployment costing $5,700 per month can deflect 10,000 support tickets, generating a return on investment of approximately 30x.
claims.suggested_rewrite: RAG operating costs and ROI vary widely, and the business case depends on usage volume and the value of the work it automates.
claims.rationale: These are synthetic business-case numbers presented as facts without a traceable source.

claims.claim_text: A 100-token chunk size yields 45% retrieval recall and poor answer quality.
claims.span_hint: section: Document Chunking, chunk-size comparison table, row 100 tokens
claims.suggested_rewrite: | 100 tokens | Variable | Often loses context | Narrow fact lookup |
claims.rationale: The exact percentages and quality labels are unsupported as presented.

claims.claim_text: A 250-token chunk size yields 72% retrieval recall and good answer quality.
claims.span_hint: section: Document Chunking, chunk-size comparison table, row 250 tokens
claims.suggested_rewrite: | 250 tokens | Variable | Often usable | Simple QA |
claims.rationale: The exact retrieval and quality figures are not grounded in an identified allowlisted source.

claims.claim_text: A 500-token chunk size yields 85% retrieval recall and the best answer quality.
claims.span_hint: section: Document Chunking, chunk-size comparison table, row 500 tokens
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **500 tokens** | Variable | Common starting point | **General use** |
claims.rationale: The table repeats unsupported exact figures from the preceding sentence.

claims.claim_text: A 1000-token chunk size yields 78% retrieval recall and good answer quality.
claims.span_hint: section: Document Chunking, chunk-size comparison table, row 1000 tokens
claims.suggested_rewrite: | 1000 tokens | Variable | Can help on broader context | Complex questions |
claims.rationale: The exact score and quality label are unsupported as written.

claims.claim_text: A 2000-token chunk size yields 65% retrieval recall and okay answer quality.
claims.span_hint: section: Document Chunking, chunk-size comparison table, row 2000 tokens
claims.suggested_rewrite: | 2000 tokens | Variable | Often too coarse for retrieval | Long-form analysis |
claims.rationale: The exact percentage and quality claim are not backed by a cited source.

claims.claim_text: LangChain provides a RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter implementation.
claims.span_hint: section: Chunking Strategies, Recursive Chunking intro
claims.rationale: The repository source directly shows LangChain's RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter implementation.

claims.claim_text: The module overstates LangChain usage intensity.
claims.span_hint: section: Chunking Strategies, Recursive Chunking intro
claims.anchor_text: Used extensively by frameworks like LangChain,
claims.suggested_rewrite: Frameworks such as LangChain provide this approach,
claims.rationale: The implementation is citable, but the unsupported 'used extensively' framing should be toned down.

claims.claim_text: A hospital in 2023 retrieved the wrong patient's medications because room metadata was stale.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories, Healthcare Chatbot
claims.anchor_text: In 2023, a hospital deployed a RAG-powered assistant to help nurses find patient information. A nurse asked: "What medications is patient in room 302 taking?" The system retrieved records and confidently listed medications. The problem: it had retrieved records for a *different* patient with a similar name who had been in room 302 three months earlier.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In healthcare-style RAG systems, semantic retrieval should be constrained by exact patient identifiers before any similarity search touches sensitive records.
claims.rationale: This reads like an unattributed incident report with precise situational detail but no verifiable source.

claims.claim_text: A law firm's RAG system missed that a cited case had been overruled because the superseding decision was in another chunk.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories, Legal Research Tool
claims.anchor_text: A law firm built a RAG system for case research. An associate cited a case from the system in a brief, only for opposing counsel to point out the case had been overruled years ago. The RAG system had retrieved the original case but missed the superseding decision because it was located in a separate chunk.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Legal research systems need authoritative status metadata so retrieved cases can be checked for whether they remain good law.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed legal war story presented as a real event without a source.

claims.claim_text: An e-commerce RAG assistant failed because users said 'comfy pants' while the catalog used more formal terms.
claims.span_hint: section: Production War Stories, E-commerce Chatbot
claims.anchor_text: An e-commerce company launched a RAG shopping assistant. A customer asking for "comfy pants for working from home" received zero results because the formal product descriptions strictly used the terms "relaxed-fit trousers" or "loungewear bottoms."
claims.suggested_rewrite: Retail search assistants often need query expansion or synonym handling when customer language differs from catalog terminology.
claims.rationale: This is a plausible composite example, but it is written as a concrete incident without attribution.

claims.claim_text: Ragas is a framework for automated evaluation of retrieval-augmented generation systems.
claims.span_hint: section: RAG Evaluation Metrics, The RAGAS Framework
claims.rationale: The Ragas paper directly supports the existence and purpose of the framework.

claims.claim_text: The module describes RAGAS as 'industry-standard' without support.
claims.span_hint: section: RAG Evaluation Metrics, The RAGAS Framework
claims.rationale: The paper supports the framework's existence, not the superlative market-position claim.

claims.claim_text: Embedding typically costs $0.0001-0.001 per 1K tokens.
claims.span_hint: section: Economics and Cost, cost table, Embedding row
claims.anchor_text: | Embedding | API calls or compute for generating embeddings | $0.0001-0.001 per 1K tokens |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Embedding | API calls or compute for generating embeddings | Varies by provider, model, and token volume |
claims.rationale: The row presents floating market pricing as a stable fact without a dated vendor source.

claims.claim_text: Vector database hosting typically costs $20-500 per month.
claims.span_hint: section: Economics and Cost, cost table, Storage row
claims.anchor_text: | Storage | Vector database hosting | $20-500/month depending on scale |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Storage | Vector database hosting | Varies with data size, retention, and replication |
claims.rationale: This pricing range is provider- and scale-dependent and is not pinned to a citable source.

claims.claim_text: LLM inference typically costs $0.001-0.06 per 1K tokens.
claims.span_hint: section: Economics and Cost, cost table, Inference row
claims.anchor_text: | Inference | LLM API calls for generation | $0.001-0.06 per 1K tokens |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Inference | LLM API calls for generation | Varies by model choice and prompt length |
claims.rationale: The row gives dated market pricing as if it were a stable cross-vendor constant.

further_reading.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
further_reading.why_relevant: The foundational RAG paper for the architecture introduced in this module.

further_reading.title: Introducing Contextual Retrieval
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for the module's sections on chunking, hybrid retrieval, and reranking tradeoffs.

further_reading.title: Ragas: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides a primary source for automated RAG evaluation concepts such as faithfulness and answer relevance.

notes: Excluded quizzes, exercises, diagrams, and code fences per pipeline rules. The chunk-size and economics tables contain multiple unsupported numeric examples that are better rewritten as variable heuristics than force-cited.
